title: Learning Without Labels
concept: Self-supervised learning (SSL) trains vision models on massive unlabeled image datasets by creating pretext puzzles, bypassing the need for human annotation.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes Swin Transformer's spatial feature map suitable for U-Net integration?

options.text: Its hierarchical output stages (H/4 to H/32) match ResNet's resolutions.
It completely avoids the query/key matrix.
It uses only one final 1D token vector.

exercises.explain: Correct! Swin's multi-stage pyramid matches CNN formats, making it easy to plug in.

cards.title: The label bottleneck
cards.body: If you want to train a model to find cars, you need humans to click on thousands of car photos and draw boxes. This is slow, expensive, and boring. What if a model could teach itself how the visual world works simply by looking at millions of unlabeled photos from the internet, just like a child learns?

cards.title: Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)
cards.body: Self-supervised learning is a training method where the model generates its own supervisory signals from unlabeled data. By solving built-in pretext tasks (like predicting parts of the image or matching distorted views), the model learns rich visual representations.

cards.title: Learning from a jigsaw puzzle
cards.body: Imagine you have a puzzle box with no picture on the cover. You don't need someone to tell you what the image is (label) to assemble it. By matching edges, colors, and textures together, you learn how the pieces fit. By the time you finish, you understand the picture's layout naturally.

cards.title: Unlabeled pre-training
cards.body: Models like DINO are pre-trained on millions of random internet images without categories. The model learns features so well that when we later give it a few labeled images of dogs and cats, it can classify them with near-perfect accuracy.

cards.title: Not unsupervised learning
cards.body: You might think SSL is the same as clustering (unsupervised learning). While both use unlabeled data, SSL uses standard supervised **loss functions** (like cross-entropy) on artificial labels it creates, acting like supervised learning.

left.text:
Requires labeled datasets
High annotation cost
Model is limited to labeled classes

right.text:
Uses raw, unlabeled images
Zero annotation cost
Learns general visual concepts

exercises.prompt: What is the primary advantage of Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)?
exercises.explain: SSL bypasses the human annotation bottleneck by using unlabeled data to learn visual representations.

options.text: It allows training on massive datasets of raw, unlabeled images without human annotations
It eliminates the need for backpropagation entirely
It forces the model to ignore color representations
It guarantees 100% accuracy on every validation set

exercises.prompt: True or false: Self-supervised learning uses supervised loss functions on artificial labels created from the data.
exercises.explain: True. SSL constructs labels automatically (e.g. comparing two crops of the same photo) to compute losses.
Fine-tuning adapts the pre-trained general feature extractor to a specific target task using a few labels.
exercises.sentence: SSL models learn general features during pre-training, which are later ___ on smaller labeled datasets.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a pretext task a self-supervised model could use to train itself?
exercises.explain: Predicting if two crops originate from the same photo is a classic pretext task (contrastive learning) used in SSL.

options.text: Predicting whether two different crops come from the same image
Manually translating image labels from English to French
Resizing an image to a fixed square resolution

exercises.prompt: Why are representations learned via SSL often more robust than those learned via standard supervised classification?

options.text: Supervised models only learn features relevant to the target labels, ignoring secondary details. SSL models learn the entire scene structure.
Because SSL models contain no learnable weights.
Because SSL models run only on raw pixel histograms.
Because SSL models ignore position embeddings.

exercises.explain: Spectacular! Supervised models cheat by focusing only on key class features (like finding a dog by its collar). SSL models must learn the whole layout to solve their puzzles. Next: contrastive learning with MoCo and SimCLR!
